turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Alito has our opinion this morning in case 16-605, Town of Chester versus Laroe Estates.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: Now, this case presents the question whether a litigant must possess Article III standing in order to intervene of right under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 24(a)(2). Land developer Steven Sherman purchased land in the town of Chester, New York, with the plan to develop a housing subdivision on the property. After years of wrangling with the town's regulatory authorities, he filed this suit in New York State Court. Among other things, he claims that the town's ever shifting body of red tape thwarted his plans for the subdivision and amounted to a regulatory taking under the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments. After the case was removed to federal district court, Laroe Estates, which is a real estate development company, and the respondent in this case, filed a motion to intervene of right under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 24(a)(2) and made allegations in an attempt to satisfy the requirements of that rule. Laroe also filed an intervener's complaint asserting a takings claim identical to Sherman's and seeking an award of compensation for the taking of Laroe's interest in the development property. The district court denied Laroe's motion to intervene on the ground that its equitable interest in the property did not confer Article III standing. The Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit reversed, holding that an intervener of right is not required to satisfy Article III's standing requirements. We granted review to address that holding. The parties agree, and now we hold, that at a minimum, an intervener of right must demonstrate Article III standing when it seeks additional relief beyond that requested by the plaintiff. That includes instances in which both the plaintiff and the intervener seek money judgments in their own names. This much follows from our settled standing precedents which made clear that, for all reliefs sought, there must be a litigant withstanding. Here, the record is unclear whether Laroe seeks relief that differs from that requested by Sherman. On remand, the Court of Appeals must resolve that ambiguity. For these reasons and others described in our opinion, we vacate the judgment of the Court of Appeals and we remand the case for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Our decision is unanimous.
